1: “On the thirteenth day of the twelfth month — the month of Adar” — the date Haman had selected by pur (lot) in Esther 3:7, casting lots from the first month (Nisan) to find the most auspicious date for destroying the Jews. The reversal is not merely that the Jews survive but that the very date chosen by supernatural divination for their destruction becomes the date of their enemies’ destruction. The pur has been turned — not cancelled but inverted by YHWH’s hidden governance.
2: The Jews assemble in their cities throughout the provinces “to strike at those who sought their harm.” Wᵉʾîš lōʾ-ʿāmaḏ bipᵉnêhem — “and no one could stand against them” — echoes Deuteronomy 11:25: “No one shall be able to stand against you.” The military-theological language of covenant warfare is invoked: the enemies of God’s people are unable to withstand them when YHWH acts on their behalf, even in a narrative that never names him.
3: Provincial officials, satraps, and governors support the Jews because pāḥaḏ mŏrᵃḏᵃkay nāpal ʿᵃlêhem — “the dread of Mordecai had fallen upon them.” The Holy War formula of divine terror (cf. Genesis 35:5; Exodus 15:16; Joshua 2:9) is here attributed to Mordecai rather than directly to God — consistent with the book’s theological method of attributing the divine action to human instruments. The officials who enforced the first decree now assist in reversing its effects.
4: Mordecai’s greatness in the palace is now empire-wide: kî-gāḏôl mŏrᵃḏᵃkay bêṯ hammelek — his reputation spreads as his authority grows. The man who sat at the gate in sackcloth (Esther 4:1) now exercises imperial-level power. The narrative draws a deliberate parallel to Joseph (Genesis 41:40-43) and Daniel (Daniel 2:48-49): the faithful Jew who refused to compromise rises from danger to power as an instrument of his people’s preservation.
5: “The Jews struck down all their enemies with the sword — killing, destroying, and doing as they pleased (kᵉriṣônām) with those who hated them.” The phrase “doing as they pleased” echoes Haman’s ambition: his genocidal plan was to do “as seems good (kᵉṭôḇ) to you” (Esther 3:11). The reversal is complete: what Haman was authorized to do to the Jews is what the Jews are now authorized to do to their attackers — but they will notably not take the plunder.
6: In Susa the citadel (bᵉšûšan habîrāh), five hundred men are killed. The capital city — site of Haman’s decree, Esther’s intercession, Mordecai’s public humiliation and exaltation — is the primary locus of both the threat and its reversal. The number is not symbolic but historical: the narrative records specific figures to ground the events in real administration of Ahasuerus’s empire.
7: The ten sons of Haman are named individually — Parshandatha, Dalphon, Aspatha — beginning the formal death record that runs through verses 7-9. Their names are Elamite or Persian compound names; scholarly analysis of the names suggests they are authentic Persian-period onomastics rather than symbolic inventions. The killing of Haman’s sons is the completion of the Agagite/Amalekite eradication that Saul failed to accomplish in 1 Samuel 15.
8: Poratha, Adalia, Aridatha — the list continues. Ancient Hebrew manuscripts and the Masoretic text write these ten names in a distinctive vertical or extended format, one name per line, to indicate their deaths in a single encounter (analogous to a casualty list). The typographic convention signals that these are not incidental casualties but the formal terminus of the Haman/Agagite line that the narrative has identified as the existential threat.
9: Parmashta, Arisai, Aridai, and Vajezatha — the final four sons. All ten sons dead, all ten named. The completeness of the list — no survivors, no heirs — closes the Agagite lineage that opened with Haman’s introduction in Esther 3:1. The narrative has been structured from the beginning toward this terminus: the failure at 1 Samuel 15 is answered here with thorough completion.
10: “The ten sons of Haman son of Hammedatha, the enemy of the Jews — they killed. But they laid no hand on the plunder (ûbabizzāh lōʾ šālᵉḥû ʾeṯ-yāḏām).” This is the first of three statements that the Jews did not take plunder (repeated in 9:15 and Esther 9:16). The restraint contrasts with Saul’s failure in 1 Samuel 15:19 (“Why did you not obey the voice of the LORD? Why did you pounce on the spoil?”). The Jews here demonstrate the covenantal discipline that their ancestor-king lacked; the Agagite threat that Saul’s greed allowed to persist is now terminated without the same moral compromise.
11: The number of killed in Susa is reported to the king on the same day — demonstrating both the efficiency of Persian administrative communication and the king’s continuing oversight of events. The report comes to him without Esther making the request; the king is informed as a matter of protocol and then turns to ask Esther whether she has any further requests.
12: The king reports to Esther: five hundred killed in Susa, the ten sons hanged. His question — “What is your request? It shall be given to you. What more is your petition? It shall be granted.” — repeats the formulaic offer of Esther 5:3, 6, and 7:2 for the fourth time. Each repetition has escalated: Esther’s request here will be for an additional day of defensive action in Susa and the public hanging of Haman’s sons on the gallows.
13: Esther’s request for an additional day in Susa reflects the practical reality that Haman’s allies and those who had organized to attack the Jews required more than one day to be neutralized in the capital. The request to hang the ten sons’ bodies on the gallows — they had already been killed — is a public legal declaration of their guilt and a deterrent warning; ancient Near Eastern practice displayed executed enemies publicly (cf. Joshua 8:29, Esther 10:26).
14: The decree is issued in Susa for the additional day; the ten sons are hanged. The narrative economy of verse 14 — two sentences for two actions — reflects that the legal and practical matters are settled. The additional day (the fourteenth of Adar in Susa vs. the thirteenth in the provinces) creates the structural basis for the two-day Purim observance: Susa celebrates on the fifteenth, the provinces on the fourteenth.
15: The Jews in Susa gather on the fourteenth of Adar and kill three hundred more men — again without taking plunder. The restraint is reiterated (ûbabizzāh lōʾ šālᵉḥû ʾeṯ-yāḏām) for the second time, reinforcing the covenantal pattern established in verse 10. The three hundred killed are those who had continued their attack after the first day; the additional day was not aggression but completion of defense.
16: The Jews throughout the provinces kill seventy-five thousand of those who hated them (bᵉśōnᵉʾîhem) — the enemies of the Jewish people who had organized to attack them under the first decree. The plunder restraint is stated a third time. The total of seventy-five thousand across the empire reflects the scale of anti-Jewish organization that had been enabled by Haman’s decree over the nine-month period since its issuance. The number is explicitly noted as not accompanied by plunder, completing the triadic emphasis on covenantal restraint.
17: The provinces celebrate on the fourteenth of Adar because their battle concluded on the thirteenth — they rested on the fourteenth and made it a day of feasting and gladness (yôm mišᵉteh wᵉśimḥāh). The spontaneous festivity precedes formal institution: the community’s natural response to deliverance creates the practice that Mordecai will subsequently codify. The celebration arises from experience before it is legislated — a pattern repeated in the institution of Passover, Pentecost, and other festivals.
18: The Jews in Susa celebrated on the fifteenth of Adar because they had assembled on both the thirteenth and fourteenth. The two-day battle creates a two-day celebration. This differential — fourteenth for the provinces, fifteenth for Susa — is the calendrical basis for the distinction between “Purim of the villages” (observed on the fourteenth) and “Purim of the walled cities” (observed on the fifteenth) in later Jewish law, where cities surrounded by walls in the time of Joshua observe on the fifteenth in honor of Susa.
19: The verse explicitly accounts for the ongoing practice: provincial Jews observe the fourteenth as “a day of gladness and feasting, a holiday, and sending portions (ûmišᵉlôaḥ māānôṯ) to one another.” The mānôṯ (portions/gifts of food) exchanged between neighbors becomes one of the defining practices of Purim observance — the communal sharing of the victory’s abundance. The practice is embedded in the observance before Mordecai’s letter formalizes it.
20: Mordecai records the events and sends letters to all the Jews throughout the empire. The shift from oral community practice to written codification is significant: the diaspora community’s religious life increasingly depended on textual transmission rather than temple presence or prophetic address. Mordecai’s letter becomes the charter document for an ongoing practice — the first festival instituted by written communal decree rather than Mosaic Torah command.
21: The establishment of Purim on the fourteenth and fifteenth of Adar every year creates a perpetual calendar anchor. Lᵉqayyēm ʿᵃlêhem — “to establish upon them” — is the legislative language of covenant obligation; the community accepts a binding annual observance. The two-day structure honors both the provincial (fourteenth) and capital (fifteenth) experiences, maintaining the historical specificity of the original events in the festival’s calendar form.
22: “As the days on which the Jews gained relief (nāḥû) from their enemies, and the month that was turned (nehpak) for them from sorrow into gladness and from mourning into a holiday.” The verb nehpak (turned/overturned) is the key theological word of the book: the pur that Haman cast to determine the day of destruction has been overturned into a day of deliverance. The festival commemorates not just the event but the turning — the divine reversal of what the enemy intended.
23: The community accepts what it had already begun to practice — the formal establishment of Purim ratifies an existing communal observance rather than imposing a new one. This pattern of legal codification following lived practice is consistent with other diaspora religious developments: practice precedes law; experience generates tradition; tradition is then ratified by communal authority. The consent structure here anticipates the rabbinic concept of minhag (custom) as a binding practice.
24: Pûr hûʾ haggôrāl — “the pur — that is, the lot.” The narrator pauses to explicitly gloss the Persian word pûr with the Hebrew gôrāl (lot), explaining the festival’s name for readers unfamiliar with Elamite/Persian vocabulary. The lot that Haman cast to destroy is the lot that now names the festival of survival. The etymology of the festival name is embedded in the reversal narrative: the very linguistic marker of Haman’s scheme becomes the permanent memorial of its failure.
25: “But when she [Esther] came before the king, he ordered by letter that Haman’s evil scheme against the Jews should fall back on his own head, and that he and his sons should be hanged on the gallows.” The summary in verse 25 is a condensed retelling of chapters 7-8, functioning as the official narrative of the decree reversal — the version that would be read in the Purim letter and transmitted to future generations. The passive “it was ordered” attributes the reversal to royal authority while the reader understands the providential agency behind it.
26: The festival is named Pûrîm — the Hebrew plural of the Persian/Elamite singular pûr. The combination of “everything written in this letter, and what they had witnessed, and what had come to them” establishes a three-source basis for Purim observance: the written record, direct experience, and received tradition. The basis of the festival is both documentary and experiential — a model for diaspora memory-formation that does not depend on physical presence at a sanctuary.
27: “The Jews established and accepted (qibbᵉlû wᵉqibbēl) for themselves, for their descendants, and for all who joined their community.” The phrase kol-hannilwîm ʿᵃlêhem — “all who had joined themselves to them” — includes proselytes and gentile sympathizers in the Purim obligation; the festival is not ethnically exclusive but extends to all who identify with the Jewish community. The verb qibbᵉlû (accepted/received) is the same root used for receiving Torah tradition — Purim observance is placed in the category of received communal obligation.
28: “These days were to be remembered (nizkarim) and kept in every generation, every family, every province, and every city.” The fourfold “every” (kol-dôr wāḏôr kol-mišpāḥāh kol-mᵉḏînāh ûmᵉḏînāh kol-ʿîr wāʿîr) emphasizes the universality of the obligation across time, kinship, geography, and settlement. The command to remember (zakōr) connects Purim to the Deuteronomic memory imperative that structures Israelite covenant theology — the festival preserves the event against the amnesia that endangers covenant fidelity.
29: Queen Esther daughter of Abihail writes with Mordecai — with “full authority (tōqep)” — to confirm the second Purim letter. Tōqep is a Persian loanword meaning force/authority/power, used of royal strength in Esther 10:2. Esther exercises legislative authority in her own right alongside Mordecai; the woman who had hidden her identity (Esther 2:10) now signs official imperial correspondence under her own name and lineage. The book ends with Esther as co-legislator of a permanent institution.
30: Mordecai sends letters to all the Jews in the hundred and twenty-seven provinces — the full extent of the empire — with words of šālôm weʾĕmeṯ (peace and truth/faithfulness). The combination echoes the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:24-26) and the covenant promises of Zechariah 8:19; the diaspora community receives an authoritative communal document that carries the weight of both royal and covenant authority.
31: The Purim observance is confirmed “as Mordecai the Jew and Queen Esther had prescribed (qiyyᵉmû ʿᵃlêhem)” — and as they had accepted for themselves and their descendants regarding the fasts and their lamenting (ṣārᵃḵêhem). The reference to fasts associated with the Purim season suggests that the three-day fast of 4:16 was institutionalized as well — the days of fasting and mourning preceding Purim serve as the counterpart to the days of feasting and joy. The liturgical year encompasses both lament and celebration.
32: “The command of Esther confirmed these regulations of Purim, and it was recorded in the book.” The final verse of the chapter ends with wᵉniktāḇ bassēper — “and it was written in the book” — closing the legislation with a citation of its written form. The book of Esther is itself the book referenced: the megillah (scroll) read annually at Purim is the record that confirms the institution. The narrative closes on its own textual authority — the scroll that documents the events is the foundation of the practice it describes.
